What qualities do you believe the Phils most needs in 2028?

If we look at the current direction of the country, I believe the Philippines would most need these qualities in 2028:

1. Moral Integrity
A leader whose character is steady even when unseen. Corruption fatigue is real. The nation needs someone who values honesty over popularity and long-term good over short-term political gain.

2. Unifying Leadership
The country has become highly polarized in recent years. A president who can calm tensions, listen across political lines, and avoid governing through division would be crucial.

3. Economic Competence
Inflation, job security, food supply, and foreign investment remain pressing issues. The next president must understand economic fundamentals and surround themselves with capable technocrats.

4. Strong but Measured Governance
Firm leadership is appreciated by many Filipinos — but strength must be balanced with respect for democratic institutions, rule of law, and human rights.

5. Foreign Policy Balance
With tensions in the West Philippine Sea and global power shifts, the Philippines needs a leader who can:
Protect national sovereignty
Maintain strategic alliances
Avoid unnecessary escalation

6. Crisis Management Ability
The Philippines is disaster-prone. The next president must be calm, data-driven, and decisive during emergencies.

7. Heart for the Poor
Millions of Filipinos still struggle daily. Policies must go beyond rhetoric and genuinely uplift those in poverty — especially in education, healthcare, and livelihood.

Naga City was selected as a pilot area for Oplan Kontra Baha due to its high vulnerability to flooding within the Bicol River Basin and its long history of severe typhoon-induced inundation. This reality underscores the urgent need for focused, sustained, and coordinated flood-mitigation efforts.

In a nation often divided by political loyalties, it is worth pausing to recognize moments of quiet maturity. One such moment is Leni Robredo’s grace in accepting electoral defeat, her respect for the Office of the President, and her continued commitment to public service—now as Mayor of Naga City.

There is an old adage in public service that leadership is revealed not in victory, but in how one conducts oneself after loss. True statesmanship lies in choosing cooperation over resentment, and service over spectacle. This principle is precisely what Mayor Robredo demonstrates.

Extending courtesy to the sitting President is neither weakness nor surrender; it is a basic expression of political maturity. Respect for institutions must transcend personalities. Unfortunately, some choose to misinterpret civility as capitulation, or coordination as endorsement.

Yes, this is political—public office always is. But not every interaction must be reduced to factional conflict. Governance cannot function if every gesture is framed as one camp versus another. Courtesy and coordination between leaders are essential elements of responsible administration, not automatic declarations of political allegiance.

In local governance, it is standard practice to accord respect to the President—ally or opponent alike. This is not merely protocol; it is statesmanship. More importantly, it provides an opportunity to advocate for programs and resources that directly benefit local communities.

Mayor Robredo has consistently upheld this standard of professionalism. She previously received Sara Duterte with courtesy during an official visit to Naga. Now that Ferdinand Marcos Jr. is visiting the city, civility and cooperation should be expected—not questioned.

When national and local leaders work together despite political differences, the real beneficiaries are the people. The recent joint inspection highlights what effective governance should look like: practical coordination, evidence-based assessment, and timely action—particularly on urgent concerns such as flood mitigation and disaster preparedness.

What stands out is the focus on substance rather than spectacle. Flood control systems, evacuation facilities, and resilient housing are not partisan issues; they are matters of public safety and human dignity. If cooperation accelerates solutions and improves outcomes, then it is not only acceptable—it is commendable.

Ultimately, good governance is not measured by who receives credit, but by whether institutions function and lives are protected. More collaboration that is quiet, results-oriented, and people-centered can only strengthen public trust and democratic institutions.

At the end of the day, public service must always come before politics. 🇵🇭

Additional Academic Trivia : Philippines is the only SE Asian county with June to March school year.

In the Know: PH only country with June-March school year

The Philippines is the only member of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (Asean) that follows a June-to-March academic calendar. Other Asean countries, including Cambodia, Laos, Indonesia, Singapore, Malaysia, Vietnam and, most recently, Thailand, are already following the September-to-May school calendar.

In the United States, the school calendar begins between August and September and ends in June.

In some European countries, the school year starts in the first week of September. In Nordic countries like Denmark, Finland, Sweden, Norway and Iceland, it generally starts between mid- to end-August. In Southern European countries like Greece, Portugal and Turkey, the school calendar starts during the second half of September.

Other Asian countries like Japan and South Korea have a different school calendar. The academic year in Japan starts in April and ends in March. South Korea’s academic year begins in March and ends in February.

Like in some European countries and in the United States, China’s school calendar also starts in September and ends in June.

In Australia, the school year begins in January and ends in December.

Executive Order No. 292, signed in July 1987 by then President Corazon Aquino, provides that the opening date of the school year for the elementary, secondary and tertiary levels for public and private schools “shall not be earlier than the first day of June nor later than the last day of July of each year unless prevented by fortuitous events.”

This academic calendar was adopted to coincide with the country’s two seasons—rainy (from June to November) and dry (from December to May).

Education Secretary Armin Luistro said last month that no decision has been made regarding the proposed shift in academic calendar for basic education.

He cited a Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical and Astronomical Services Administration (Pagasa) study showing that typhoons and heavy rains are not frequent occurrences during June and July.

Sources: Inquirer Archives, Organisation of School Time in Europe by the European Commission, Seoul National University, Chuo University, Australia.gov.au, NYC Department of Education

Topacio's "illegal" rhetoric is textbook misdirection—he's framing withdrawal from Rome Statute as some kind of sovereignty shield, when even basic treaty law under Article 127 explicitly rejects that defense. Ang galing ng timing: scream "void!" loud enough, baka hindi mapansin na the ICC literally rejected this exact argument multiple times already, and Duterte's sitting in The Hague right now precisely because that legal Houdini act didn't work.
What's tragically brilliant about this strategy is how it weaponizes nationalism to protect impunity. By repackaging accountability as "foreign interference," they're betting Filipinos will feel more insulted by international scrutiny than by 20,000+ extrajudicial killings na halos walang kaso na nagseryoso. It's the ultimate shell game: make the court the villain, so walang titingin dun sa actual bodies, sa mga "nanlaban" reports na copypaste, sa systemic pattern ng Davao Death Squad methodology na nag-scale nationwide.
The real tell? Topacio's outrage is surgically selective. Galit na galit sa ICC indictment, pero yung mass graves worth of victims? Yung families na umiyak sa Senate hearings? Yung police template reports? Dead silence. That's not lawyering—that's PR for a killing machine pretending to be policy.
At yung irony na totally lost sa kanila: they're screaming about "sovereignty" while proving exactly why complementarity failed. The ICC exists because domestic systems won't touch their own elites. Kung nag-function talaga yung accountability dito, walang The Hague case. They created the very vacuum they're now crying about, tapos may audacity pang mag-victim.
Bato and Go aren't "co-perpetrators" dahil lang sa proximity—they're there because one architected the operational framework (Double Barrel) and the other was the connective tissue sa apparatus na nag-sustain ng buong operation. That's not "tagging," yan yung pagiging part ng machine mismo.
The saddest part? Topacio could've pushed for genuine domestic trials, used his platform para sabihin "tara, litisin natin dito para hindi na tayo umabot sa ICC." Instead, he chose to be the legal face of "better no justice than foreign justice"—and that calculus only works kung ang mga pinatay mo ay hindi ka botante, hindi ka may boses, hindi ka counted.

What strikes me most is yung "on/off switch" observation. That's the smoking gun, di ba? Kasi if this was really just "rogue elements" or "overzealous cops" like they claim, bakit synchronized ang violence with policy directives? Bakit may correlation between official campaigns and "vigilante" killings? That's not chaos—that's orchestration.
And yung layers of deniability—ang galing ng breakdown. Each co-perpetrator operated sa sweet spot nila: Bato institutionalized it through doctrine, Go maintained political cover and managed access (the "nervous system" analogy is chef's kiss), Aguirre weaponized prosecutorial discretion by choosing what not to investigate. Classic bureaucratic violence—walang direktang utos on paper, pero lahat nag-align sa isang direction through incentives, signals, and institutional pressure.
Pero here's what haunts me: yung question at the end about safeguards. Kasi the scariest part isn't just that this happened—it's how easy it was to execute using existing structures. Hindi na kailangan ng bagong death squad infrastructure eh. The PNP, DOJ, NBI, PDEA—lahat nandyan na. You just need the right people in the right positions who understand the assignment.
This is why accountability matters beyond just "justice for victims." It's about creating jurisprudential and institutional memory that makes the next would-be strongman think twice. Kung walang consequences, it's just a playbook for the next one.

Ah, the classic "bakit wala si Sara?" question — and honestly, it's the right question to ask, kasi ang daming tao akala nila co-perpetrator siya automatically just because she's a Duterte and former Davao mayor. But here's the real tea:
Si Sara, nasa waiting room pa lang, hindi pa siya sa main event.
The ICC didn't exclude her because she's innocent or protected — they excluded her from this specific charging document because that's how strategic prosecution works. Think of it like building a case laban sa drug cartel: you don't go after everyone agad-agad. You start with the kingpin (Rodrigo) and his clear command structure (PNP chiefs, DOJ officials, the people with actual paper trails showing direct orders).
Sara's situation is more nuanced — she's what they call a "secondary respondent," which sounds softer pero in reality, it just means "later, but not forgotten."
Here's why that matters:
1. Documentary evidence is king sa ICC. The current DCC focuses on the national drug war machinery — yung may clear chain of command from Malacañang down to the tokhang operations. Sara's alleged involvement is more localized (Davao-specific), and yung continuity ng Davao Death Squad template under her watch requires a different evidentiary package. Hindi pwedeng sabay lang i-lump in kung hindi pa solid yung build-up.
2. The ICC plays chess, not checkers. They don't blow their entire load sa first charge sheet. They secure the main targets muna, then expand outward. Sara being "on the horizon" doesn't mean she's safe — it means the prosecution is pacing itself. Kumbaga, si Duterte ang unang barko na babagsak; si Sara, sumunod na alon yan.
3. Her own statements are already being used against her family. The ICC literally cited Sara's public remarks (plus Baste's and Rodrigo's) to reject Duterte Sr.'s interim release plea. So she's already materially involved in the case — just not yet as a named co-perpetrator. That's a very thin line, and it can shift anytime new evidence surfaces or the prosecution decides to file amended charges.
4. "Secondary respondent" is legalese for "you're next in line." Walang ganap na safe space dito. The moment the ICC establishes the Davao template as the blueprint for the national drug war, Sara's role as Davao mayor during key years becomes extremely relevant. And unlike her father, she doesn't have the luxury of claiming she was "just following orders" — she was the local executive, the one allegedly continuing the very system that the ICC is prosecuting.
So bakit importante 'to?
Because Filipinos love false binaries: either you're charged or you're cleared. But international criminal justice doesn't work that way. Sara not being on the initial list doesn't mean vindication — it means strategic sequencing. The ICC is building a case that can actually stick, not just throwing spaghetti at the wall.
And here's the kicker: she herself said she believes she's on the ICC's radar. That's not paranoia — that's someone who knows exactly what kind of receipts exist. Kaya nga walang kampante dito. The fact that she's publicly acknowledging it tells you she's preparing for the possibility, which is very different from the posture of someone who genuinely believes they're in the clear.
Bottom line: Sara's absence from this list is procedural, not substantive. The ICC is building a ladder, and right now, they're focused on the top rungs — yung mga direktang nag-design at nag-execute ng drug war machinery. But make no mistake: yung buong ladder, tinitingnan nila. Sara is still part of the structure, still part of the political and operational continuity, and still very much within the scope of accountability.
Kaya ang tanong hindi dapat "bakit wala siya?" — ang tanong dapat ay "kailan siya?" Because in cases like this, it's rarely a matter of if, but when. #LakaFuerte

Oo nga, "justice delayed" daw—pero ang totoo, justice wasn't just delayed, pinatulog muna kasi puyat tayong lahat sa gaslighting.
What hits different about this ICC development is how it dismantles the very defense mechanism that kept this machinery oiled for years: yung narrative na "one bad apple," "rogue cops," or "misunderstood intentions." The Document Containing the Charges doesn't just point fingers—it connects the dots from Davao to Malacañang, from street-level executions to Cabinet-level complicity. It names names. And suddenly, "sumunod lang" transforms from alibi to admission of guilt.
The genius—if you can call it that—of Duterte's war on drugs was that it weaponized our collective exhaustion with crime and dressed up mass murder as decisive governance. Kaya nga maraming nagsabi, "eh at least may ginagawa." But the ICC is now doing what our own institutions couldn't or wouldn't: treating the system as the crime scene, not just the corpses.
Ang ironic: the same people who screamed "sovereignty" when we withdrew from the ICC are now facing the reality that international law doesn't care about your press releases. You can't "unsubscribe" from crimes against humanity like it's a newsletter. The court's jurisdiction locked in the moment the alleged crimes began, and no amount of political theater changes that.
But here's what terrifies me: even with all this—ICC warrants, named co-perpetrators, a confirmation hearing on the calendar—there are still those who will frame this as "destabilization," as "NPA propaganda," as anything except accountability for thousands of extrajudicial killings. Kasi once you admit the drug war was murder dressed as policy, you have to reckon with the fact that we let it happen. Some of us even cheered it on.
So yes, justice may be slow. Pero ang mas masakit? Justice is also a mirror. And not everyone is ready to look. #LakaFuerte
